flashcards.back: Encapsulation is the bundling of data (variables) and methods (functions) into a single unit (class), restricting direct access to some components to prevent accidental modification.
Abstraction is the process of hiding complex implementation details and showing only the essential features of an object to the user.
Inheritance is a mechanism where a class (derived class) can inherit properties and behaviors from another class (base class), promoting code reusability.
Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to be treated as objects of a common superclass. It enables one interface to be used for different data types.

flashcards.front: What is a Class in OOP?
flashcards.back: A class is a blueprint or template that defines the attributes (data members) and methods (functions) that objects created from the class will have.

flashcards.front: What is an Object in OOP?
flashcards.back: An object is an instance of a class that contains real values (actual data) and can perform the operations defined in its class.
A constructor is a special method called automatically when an object is created, used to initialize the object's state/attributes.
A destructor is a special method called when an object is destroyed or goes out of scope, used to release resources or perform cleanup operations.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between public, private, and protected access specifiers?
flashcards.back: Public: accessible from anywhere; Private: accessible only within the class; Protected: accessible within class and derived classes.
Single inheritance occurs when a class inherits from only one parent class, forming a simple inheritance hierarchy.
Multiple inheritance occurs when a class inherits from more than one parent class, allowing a derived class to have features of multiple base classes.
Method overriding allows a derived class to provide a specific implementation of a method already defined in its base class.
Method overloading allows multiple methods in the same class to have the same name but different parameters (different signatures).

flashcards.front: What is a Pure Virtual Function?
flashcards.back: A pure virtual function is a virtual function declared in a base class with no implementation, forcing derived classes to provide their own implementation.

flashcards.front: What is an Interface in OOP?
flashcards.back: An interface is a contract that defines a set of method signatures without implementation. Classes implementing an interface must provide implementations for all defined methods.
